sporsmal.forklaring: Release the muscles and let the breath do the work — find a comfortable edge and stay there.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why is savasana important?
valg: The body absorbs the work and sinks fully into calm
sporsmal.forklaring: Savasana lets the nervous system digest the session and sink into deep calm — never skip it.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How should the calm poses feel?
valg: A gentle stretch and rest, never sharp pain
sporsmal.forklaring: This is rest and a gentle stretch — take it easy and breathe calmly.

kilder.kilde: Benson – “The Relaxation Response” (Harvard)
kilder.om: Describes how calm breathing and rest trigger a measurable relaxation response in the body.

kilder.kilde: Bernie Clark – “The Complete Guide to Yin Yoga”
kilder.om: A foundational book on the calm, long holds and how they affect the body and nervous system.

steg.blurb: Sit back on your heels with your knees apart, stretch your arms forward and lower your chest toward the floor.
Lie on your back, bring both knees to one side and stretch your arms out. Let your gaze go the opposite way.

sjekk.sporsmal: How do you take a gentle twist?
valg: Let your knees drop to the side and breathe calmly
sjekk.forklaring: Let your knees drop gently to the side and let gravity and the breath do the work.

steg.blurb: Lie on your stomach, set your forearms on the floor under your shoulders and lift your chest into a mild, comfortable backbend.
Sit with the soles of your feet together and your knees out to the sides. Lean gently forward with a rounded back and let your hips release.
Sit with your legs stretched out in front and fold gently forward with a rounded back. Let everything hang heavy and breathe deeply.
Lie completely still on your back with your arms and legs relaxed. Let your breath be natural and your body heavy.

sjekk.sporsmal: What do you do in savasana?
valg: Lie completely still and let the body sink into calm
sjekk.forklaring: Lie completely still, let your breath be natural and your body heavy — and get up slowly afterward.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Lie on your back and take ten calm breaths.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Stay lying in savasana and get up slowly.

enheter.intro: The longest, deepest holds in yin yoga — where you let go completely and let time and gravity do the work.

teori.undertittel: How long, calm holds reach deep into connective tissue, joints and stillness.

avsnitt.p: Yin yoga is the quiet counterpart to the flowing yang yoga. Instead of moving and using your muscles, you sink into a pose and stay there for a long time — often two to five minutes. It's a deep, meditative practice.

The long holds target the connective tissue — ligaments, tendons and fascia — which doesn't respond to quick movements, but gives way gradually under a sustained, mild pull over time. That's why we hold long and calm, instead of pushing hard for a short while.

The key in yin is releasing muscle tension so gravity can do the work. Find about 60–70% of your maximal pull — never sharp pain — and stay there without adjusting. Feel free to use pillows and blankets for support so you can fully let go.

The long holds also challenge the mind: staying still when your thoughts want to move on. Use the breath as an anchor, notice the sensations without judging them, and let yourself sink. Yin trains both the tissue and the ability to rest in stillness.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What do the long yin holds target?
valg: Connective tissue like ligaments, tendons and fascia
sporsmal.forklaring: Yin works on the connective tissue, which yields under a long, mild pull — not quick movements.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How intense should a yin hold be?
valg: Around 60–70% of max, never sharp pain
sporsmal.forklaring: Find a mild edge (~60–70%), relax the muscle and stay there — never sharp pain.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the typical hold time in yin?
sporsmal.forklaring: Yin holds usually last 2–5 minutes, so the connective tissue has time to yield.

kilder.kilde: Paul Grilley – “Yin Yoga: Principles and Practice”
kilder.om: The founder of modern yin yoga explains how long holds affect connective tissue and joints.

kilder.kilde: Bernie Clark – “The Complete Guide to Yin Yoga”
kilder.om: Elaborates on the principles of time, mild load and the use of props in yin practice.

steg.blurb: Sit between your heels and lean back gently onto your hands or elbows. Feel free to place a cushion along your back for support.

sjekk.sporsmal: What's smart in deep yin poses like saddle?
valg: Using pillows and blankets for support
sjekk.forklaring: Props let you fully let go and sink safely into the hold.

steg.blurb: Lie on your stomach and press your torso up onto straight arms into a deep but comfortable backbend. Let your glutes relax.
From your back: roll your legs over your head toward the floor behind you, with good support under your shoulders. Ease out carefully if it's uncomfortable.
From all fours: bring one knee forward toward the same hand and stretch the other leg back. Lower your hips and lean forward.
A deep lunge with your rear knee on the floor. Sink your hips forward and down, and let your front hip open.
From all fours: slide your knees wide out to the sides with your ankles in line with your knees, and lower your hips slowly.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Start in child's pose and take ten deep breaths.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Finish in savasana and let your whole body sink.

undertittel: What mobility really is — and how to move your neck, shoulders and arms with control.

enheter.intro: First of all: what separates mobility from stretching? Learn the difference, and meet CARs — the controlled joint circles that are at the heart of all mobility training.

teori.tittel: Mobility is strength at end range
teori.undertittel: The difference between mobility, flexibility and warming up — and why it's worth understanding.

avsnitt.p: Many people confuse stretching and mobility, but they aren't the same. Flexibility is how far a joint can move passively — how far someone can push you. Mobility is how far you can move the joint yourself actively, with control and strength. Mobility is simply usable flexibility.

You can be flexible without having mobility: a split helps little if you don't have the strength to control your leg out there on your own. Mobility training builds exactly that strength at end range, so you can actually use your range of motion — and protect your joints from injury.

The basic tool is CARs (Controlled Articular Rotations): large, slow, controlled circles with one joint at a time, as far as you can go in every direction. You brace your body lightly, move only the joint you're working on, and own every millimeter of the circle. CARs keep your joints healthy, lubricated and under your control.

We train the body joint by joint — neck, shoulders, spine, hips, knees and ankles — because each joint has its own needs. Some joints (like the hips and thoracic spine) need mostly mobility, others (like the lower back and knees) need mostly stability. This unit starts with breath and neck; then we build our way down the body.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the difference between flexibility and mobility?
valg: Flexibility is passive range; mobility is active, controlled use of it
sporsmal.forklaring: Flexibility is how far you can be pushed; mobility is how far you can actively control the joint yourself.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What are CARs?
valg: Controlled joint circles — large, slow rotations of one joint
sporsmal.forklaring: CARs are Controlled Articular Rotations: slow, controlled circles with one joint at a time.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why isn't passive stretching enough on its own?
valg: You lack the strength to use and control the end range
Because joints can't become more mobile
sporsmal.forklaring: Without active strength at end range you can't use your flexibility safely — that's what mobility builds.

kilder.kilde: Gray Cook & Michael Boyle – the “Joint-by-Joint” model
kilder.om: The framework that each joint in the body has a different need for either mobility or stability.

kilder.kilde: Andreo Spina – Functional Range Conditioning (CARs)
kilder.om: The origin of CARs as a method: controlled joint rotations for joint health and active movement control.

steg.blurb: Lie on your back with one hand on your belly. Breathe calmly down into your belly so it rises, and let the exhale be long.

sjekk.sporsmal: Where should you feel the breath in belly breathing?
valg: Down into the belly, which rises
sjekk.forklaring: Breathe down into your belly so it rises — that calms the nervous system and gives a stable core.

steg.blurb: Sit or stand tall. Draw big, slow circles with your head — as far as you can go in each direction, with full control.

sjekk.sporsmal: How do you do a CAR correctly?
valg: Slowly and controlled through the whole movement
As fast and far as possible
With a jerk at the end
sjekk.forklaring: CARs should be slow and controlled — you own the whole circle, with no jerking.

steg.blurb: On all fours: nod your head slowly up and down, and let your gaze lead. It wakes up the deep control of the neck.
On all fours: turn your head slowly from side to side and let your gaze follow. Soft, controlled rotation.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Lie on your back and take five calm belly breaths.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Stay lying down and breathe calmly for a moment.

enheter.intro: The shoulder is the body's most mobile joint — and the one that stiffens up most easily. Here we take it through its entire range.

teori.undertittel: Why the shoulder needs active control through its whole large range of motion.

avsnitt.p: The shoulder (a ball-and-socket joint) can move in almost every direction — that makes it wonderfully flexible, but also vulnerable. A daily life in front of a screen tends to give hunched-forward, stiff shoulders. The goal here is to bring back the full range and control over it.

Shoulder CARs take the joint through its entire range: forward, up, back and down in one big, controlled circle. Keep the rest of your body still and move only your arm from the shoulder. This maps where you're stiff, and builds control exactly there.

Much of shoulder health lies in rotation — the ability to turn the upper arm inward and outward. Lift-off exercises (lifting your hands behind your back) and shoulder dislocates with a band train exactly this, and counter the internally rotated, slumped posture.

Furthest out sit the elbows and wrists, which few people train deliberately. A few simple CARs here keep them strong and supple — important for everything from carries to planks and pressing. Strong wrists are underrated mobility.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What do shoulder CARs take the joint through?
valg: The whole range of motion in one controlled circle
sporsmal.forklaring: Shoulder CARs take the joint through its entire range — forward, up, back and down with control.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What do lift-offs and shoulder dislocates especially train?
sporsmal.forklaring: They train internal and external rotation — the key to a healthy, open shoulder.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why move only the arm in a shoulder CAR?
valg: To isolate the joint and own the control
To go as fast as possible
sporsmal.forklaring: Keep everything else still and isolate the shoulder — that's how you build real control in the joint.

kilder.kilde: Andreo Spina – Functional Range Conditioning (shoulder CARs)
kilder.om: CARs methodology applied to the shoulder joint to preserve and build an active range of motion.

kilder.kilde: Kelly Starrett – “Becoming a Supple Leopard”
kilder.om: Practical work on shoulder rotation and posture for health and performance.

steg.blurb: Move one arm in a big, slow circle from the shoulder — forward, up, back and down. Keep the rest of your body still.

sjekk.sporsmal: What should stay still during a shoulder CAR?
valg: The rest of the body — only the arm moves
sjekk.forklaring: Isolate the shoulder: keep your core and hips still and move only the arm from the shoulder joint.

steg.blurb: Hold a band wide and bring it slowly overhead and down behind your back, then back again. Go wider if it feels tight.
Lie on your stomach with your arms out in a T or W, thumbs up. Lift your hands off the floor by rotating your shoulders outward.
Bring your hands behind your lower back, backs of the hands against your back. Lift them away from your back by rotating your shoulders.
Draw slow circles with your elbows, and then with your wrists — full control at the end ranges of the movement.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Roll your shoulders and take a few belly breaths.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Shake out your arms and breathe calmly.

undertittel: A supple spine and a stable core — the engine for all safe movement.

enheter.intro: A stiff thoracic spine steals movement from the neck, shoulders and lower back. We wake the spine up segment by segment.

teori.undertittel: Why the thoracic spine should be mobile, and the lower back stable.

avsnitt.p: The spine isn't one stiff column, but many small joints that should each move a little. If you sit a lot, the thoracic spine (the upper part) stiffens up especially, and then the neck and lower back have to take the strain. Giving the thoracic spine its movement back eases the pressure everywhere.

avsnitt.h: Mobile thoracic spine, stable lower back

avsnitt.p: According to joint-by-joint thinking, the thoracic spine should be mobile, while the lower back should be stable. So we chase rotation and flexion in the thoracic spine, but train the lower back to stay calm and steady. Forcing movement out of a lower back that would rather be stable is a common source of pain.

Cat-cow and spinal CARs teach you to roll through the spine one joint at a time, instead of bending everything from one place. Take it slow and notice where the movement skips over stiff spots — that's where you have the most to gain.

avsnitt.h: All four are the starting point

avsnitt.p: Much of this is done on all fours, where the back is unloaded and you can easily isolate the movement. Rocking and the crawling position wake up the coordination between shoulders, back and hips — the foundational patterns the body is built for.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Which part of the back should be most mobile?
sporsmal.forklaring: The thoracic spine should be mobile; the lower back should instead be stable.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What happens if the thoracic spine is stiff?
valg: The neck and lower back have to take the strain
sporsmal.forklaring: A stiff thoracic spine forces the neighboring joints to overcompensate — often the source of pain.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the point of moving the spine segment by segment?
valg: Finding and loosening stiff spots you'd otherwise skip
sporsmal.forklaring: If you roll joint by joint, you find the stiff spots instead of bending everything from one place.

kilder.kilde: Gray Cook & Michael Boyle – the “Joint-by-Joint” model
kilder.om: Describes how the thoracic spine should prioritize mobility and the lower back stability.

kilder.kilde: Original Strength (Tim Anderson) – rolling, rocking and crawling
kilder.om: Foundational floor patterns that rebuild the coordination between back, shoulders and hips.

steg.blurb: Roll slowly through your spine one segment at a time — flexion, rotation and extension with full control.
On all fours: round your back toward the ceiling on the exhale, drop your belly and open your chest on the inhale. Move segment by segment.

sjekk.sporsmal: What is the key in cat-cow for the spine?
valg: Bending everything from the lower back
sjekk.forklaring: Roll through each segment instead of bending everything from one point.

steg.blurb: On your hands and toes with your knees just above the floor and your head up. Hold a steady, controlled position.
On all fours: rock gently back and forth toward your heels with a long back. It wakes up the hips and shoulders together.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few easy cat-cows to wake up the back.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Rest in child's pose and breathe calmly.

enheter.intro: Mobility without a stable core is like firing a cannon from a canoe. Here we build the support that lets you move safely.

teori.undertittel: Why a strong, stable core is the prerequisite for good mobility.

avsnitt.p: It sounds backwards in a mobility module, but stability and mobility are two sides of the same coin. The body only releases movement it feels it can control. A strong, stable core is the security that lets the hips and back move freely.

Think of the core as a cylinder around your middle — abs, sides, back and pelvic floor — that keeps the spine steady while the arms and legs work. It should be braced in moderation and work with the breath, not locked hard. Good core control protects your lower back in everything you do.

The best core exercises are about resisting movement, not creating it. The bird-dog teaches you to keep your back still while your arms and legs extend; the side plank resists the body falling sideways; the curl-up engages your abs without straining your back. These are McGill's well-known Big 3.

The key is to breathe calmly even while you hold. If you can talk and breathe evenly in a bird-dog or side plank, you have real, usable stability — not just a tense, breath-held second. Calm strength is the goal.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What kind of core exercises are most effective?
valg: The ones with the most ab exercises
The ones that bend the lower back the most
sporsmal.forklaring: Real core strength is about resisting movement — keeping the back steady while the limbs work.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why is stability important for mobility?
valg: The body only releases movement it can control
sporsmal.forklaring: A stable core gives the security that lets the hips and back move freely.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What should you be able to do while holding a bird-dog?
sporsmal.forklaring: Calm breathing during the hold shows you have real, usable stability.

kilder.kilde: Dr. Stuart McGill – the “Big 3” (curl-up, side plank, bird-dog)
kilder.om: Research-based core exercises that build spinal stability without straining the lower back.

kilder.kilde: McGill – “Back Mechanic” / University of Waterloo
kilder.om: The principle of building spinal stability through anti-movement and good breathing.

steg.blurb: On all fours: extend the opposite arm and leg into one long line, keep your back completely still, and draw them back in with control.

sjekk.sporsmal: What should stay still in the bird-dog?
sjekk.forklaring: Keep your back and pelvis completely still while the arm and leg extend — that's the whole point.

steg.blurb: Lie on your back with your hands under your lower back and one knee bent. Lift your head and shoulders slightly and brace your abs — without flattening your back.
From your side: stack yourself in one straight line on your forearm and feet (or knees), and lift your hips. Resist your body sagging.
Lie on your back and roll slowly onto your stomach led by one arm, without pushing off with your legs. It builds coordinated core control.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few cat-cows and belly breaths to wake up the core.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Roll up slowly and breathe out.

undertittel: The body's power center: open, strong hips that move in every direction.

enheter.intro: The hip is the body's most complex joint. We take it through its full range — circles, rotation and control.

teori.undertittel: Why stiff hips are felt everywhere — and how to open them with control.

avsnitt.p: The hip is a large ball-and-socket joint that should move in every direction: flexion, extension, out to the side, in, and rotation both ways. When the hips stiffen up — often from a lot of sitting — you feel it in your lower back, knees and the whole way you move. Free hips are perhaps the best investment in mobility you can make.

Most people can flex and extend the hip, but it's rotation — turning the thigh inward and outward — that's usually missing. The 90/90 position, where both knees are bent at right angles, is the classic way to train exactly that internal and external rotation of the hips.

Hip CARs take the joint through its entire range, slowly and under control, while you stand firm on the other leg. It shows you where you're stiff, and builds active strength right out there at the end range — the usable mobility.

The hip airplane and 90/90 switches take you from passive stretching to active control: you move in and out of the end range under your own power. That's how you turn a static flexibility into something you can actually use when you run, lift and play.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Which hip movement do people most often lack?
valg: Rotation — turning the thigh in and out
sporsmal.forklaring: Most people have flexion and extension; it's rotation that's often missing — that's why we do the 90/90 work.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does the 90/90 position especially train?
valg: Internal and external rotation of the hips
sporsmal.forklaring: 90/90 with both knees at right angles is the classic way to train hip rotation.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What do hip CARs do for you?
valg: Maps stiffness and builds strength at the end range
Only warms up the heart rate
sporsmal.forklaring: CARs show where you're stiff and build active control out there at end range.

kilder.kilde: Andreo Spina – Functional Range Conditioning (hip CARs, 90/90)
kilder.om: CARs and 90/90 methodology for active hip rotation and joint health.

kilder.kilde: Gray Cook – hip mobility and movement screening
kilder.om: Shows how hip mobility affects the lower back, knees and movement quality.

steg.blurb: Stand firm on one leg and guide the other knee in a big, slow circle from the hip — up, out, back and home. Keep your torso still.

sjekk.sporsmal: What do you keep still during a hip CAR?
valg: The torso and the standing leg
sjekk.forklaring: Stand firm and keep your torso still — isolate the movement to the hip joint.

steg.blurb: Stand or sit and rotate your thighs slowly inward and outward. Feel the hip joint turn in both directions.
Sit with your front and back knees bent at 90 degrees. Sit tall and lean gently toward the front leg.
Sit in 90/90 and swing both knees slowly over to the opposite side, again and again, with control. Active hip rotation.

sjekk.sporsmal: What do 90/90 switches train?
valg: Active rotation in and out of the hips
sjekk.forklaring: You move actively in and out of the rotation with your own control — usable hip mobility.

steg.blurb: Stand on one leg in a hip hinge and rotate your pelvis open and closed over the standing leg, with control. Demanding balance and control.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few hip rotations and a round of cat-cow.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Sit in 90/90 and breathe out calmly.

enheter.intro: Now we turn hip mobility into deep, strong movement: deep squats, lateral movement and the big full-body stretch.

teori.undertittel: How deep squats and active lunges build mobility you can stand and move in.

avsnitt.p: Being able to sit in a deep squat with your heels on the floor is one of the human body's natural resting positions — but many people have lost it. Getting it back opens the hips, knees and ankles at once, and gives you a strong, usable bottom position.

avsnitt.h: Build time in the bottom position

avsnitt.p: The deep squat hold is about spending time at the bottom: relaxing the hips, keeping the chest lifted and breathing calmly. A little every day, and your body gets used to the position again. Feel free to hold onto a doorframe at first if balance is difficult.

The Cossack squat takes you from side to side in a deep wide stance and opens the inner thighs and hips in a way straight squats don't. It builds both flexibility and strength at end range — exactly what mobility is about.

The world's greatest stretch and the pigeon push-up combine several joints in one flow: hip opening, thoracic rotation and a stretch on the back side, all with active control. Such compound movements give a lot of mobility in a short time, and remind the body that the joints are meant to work together.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does the deep squat hold build?
valg: A strong, usable bottom position with open hips, knees and ankles
sporsmal.forklaring: Time in the deep squat opens hips, knees and ankles and brings back a natural resting position.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does the Cossack squat especially open?
valg: The inner thighs and hips, from side to side
sporsmal.forklaring: The lateral movement in the Cossack squat opens the adductors and hips in a way straight squats don't.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the benefit of compound exercises like the world's greatest stretch?
valg: They train several joints together with active control
sporsmal.forklaring: Compound movements give a lot of mobility in a short time and teach the joints to work together.

kilder.kilde: Ido Portal / movement culture – the deep squat
kilder.om: Popularized the deep resting squat as a daily mobility practice.

kilder.kilde: Kelly Starrett – “Becoming a Supple Leopard”
kilder.om: Practical work with deep positions, lunges and compound mobility exercises.

steg.blurb: Squat down deep with your heels on the floor, chest lifted and elbows against the insides of your knees. Breathe calmly and relax your hips.

sjekk.sporsmal: What is the goal in the deep squat hold?
valg: Relaxing deep down with the heels on the floor
sjekk.forklaring: Stay down deep, heels on the floor, and let your hips relax — build time in the bottom position.

steg.blurb: Stand wide and shift your weight deeply onto one bent leg while the other stays straight. Move slowly from side to side.
From a deep lunge: place your hand down, rotate your torso open toward the ceiling, and reach. It opens the hip, thoracic spine and posterior chain in one movement.
In pigeon pose (front knee bent, back leg extended): press your torso up and down with your hands and open the hip actively.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few hip rotations and one world's greatest stretch per side.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Sit in a deep squat and breathe calmly.

undertittel: Ankles, knees and the ability to get down to the floor and back up with ease.

enheter.intro: Furthest down sit the ankles and knees — and the ability to rise from the floor without your hands is a true measure of full-body mobility.

teori.undertittel: Why ankle mobility and standing up without hands say the most about your mobility.

avsnitt.p: The ankle is a small joint with big importance. Stiff ankles steal depth from your squat, push your knees inward and worsen your balance. A little deliberate ankle work pays off in everything you do standing.

avsnitt.h: Knees need both movement and security

avsnitt.p: The knee is essentially a hinge joint that flexes and extends, and otherwise wants to be stable. Knee and ankle CARs keep these joints lubricated and healthy through their natural range, without forcing rotation where it doesn't belong.

avsnitt.h: Standing up is mobility in practice

avsnitt.p: Getting down to the floor and back up again without using your hands is one of the most practical skills there is — research even links it to how long people live well. It requires hip, knee and ankle mobility, balance and strength, all at once.

avsnitt.h: Cross-body patterns tie the body together

avsnitt.p: The cross-crawl march — hand to opposite knee — wakes up the coordination between upper and lower body across the body. Such cross-body patterns are how we walk, run and move naturally, and they're a nice way to round off full-body mobility.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why is ankle mobility important?
valg: Stiff ankles steal depth and push the knees inward
sporsmal.forklaring: Good ankle mobility gives deeper squats, safer knees and better balance.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does standing up without hands tell you about?
valg: Full-body mobility, balance and strength combined
sporsmal.forklaring: Getting up from the floor without hands requires hip, knee and ankle mobility, balance and strength all at once.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does the cross-crawl march wake up?
valg: The cross-body coordination between upper and lower body
sporsmal.forklaring: Cross-body patterns connect the upper and lower body — the foundation for walking and running naturally.

kilder.kilde: Brito et al. – “Sitting-Rising Test” (Eur. J. Prev. Cardiol., 2014)
kilder.om: A study linking the ability to rise from the floor without support to movement health and longevity.

kilder.kilde: Original Strength (Tim Anderson) – cross-body patterns
kilder.om: Cross-body movements that rebuild the coordination between upper and lower body.

steg.blurb: Draw slow, controlled circles with your ankle, and bend/straighten your knee through its full range. Full control at the end ranges.
Stand and bring your hand to the opposite, lifted knee — alternating, at an easy pace. A cross-body pattern for the whole body.
From kneeling: rise to standing and back down again without using your hands. Pure hip, knee and balance control.

sjekk.sporsmal: What does standing up without hands require?
valg: Hip, knee and ankle mobility, balance and strength
sjekk.forklaring: Standing up without hands brings together the whole body's mobility, balance and strength in one movement.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few ankle CARs and a deep squat hold.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Shake out your legs and breathe calmly.

undertittel: What strength training is, how it works, and the principle that makes you grow.

enheter.intro: First of all: what happens in the body when you lift, and why are free weights such a good way to get strong? We start by mobilizing for the lift.

teori.tittel: Strength is built on five movements
teori.undertittel: What strength training does to your body — and why free weights give the most back.

avsnitt.p: Strength training doesn't just make you stronger. It builds muscle, makes your bones denser, keeps your metabolism up and protects you against injury and the decline of aging. Adults should train strength at least two days a week — it's one of the most valuable things you can do for your health. This is the first lesson in the strength school.

When you lift, two adaptations happen. First the nervous system learns to use the muscles you already have more efficiently — that's why you get stronger quickly in the first weeks without getting much bigger. Then the muscles build up and grow. Both come from challenging the body a little more over time.

Dumbbells, kettlebells and the barbell let you move freely through the full range of motion, and force the body to stabilize itself. That builds strength you can use in daily life and sport, not just in one machine. In return they require technique — which you'll learn here, step by step.

Almost all strength is built through five patterns: the squat (bending at the legs), the hip hinge (bending at the hips), the press (pushing away), the pull (pulling toward you) and the carry (holding and walking). Master these, and you cover the whole body. We take one pattern at a time — but first we mobilize the body for lifting.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What are the two adaptations that make you stronger?
valg: The nervous system becomes more efficient, and the muscles grow
Only that you become more flexible
sporsmal.forklaring: First your neural control improves (a rapid strength gain), then the muscles grow.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why free weights over machines alone?
valg: A full range of motion, and the body has to stabilize itself
sporsmal.forklaring: Free weights give a full range and train stabilization — usable strength for daily life and sport.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What are the five basic movements?
valg: Squat, hinge, press, pull and carry
Running, jumping, throwing, swimming and walking
sporsmal.forklaring: Squat, hip hinge, press, pull and carry together cover the whole body.

kilder.kilde: American College of Sports Medicine – position stand on strength training (2009)
kilder.om: Recommends strength training at least two days a week and describes the basic principles for healthy adults.

kilder.kilde: Mark Rippetoe – “Starting Strength”
kilder.om: A standard reference on why compound free-weight exercises build strength effectively, and how to learn the technique.

steg.blurb: Hold a light kettlebell or dumbbell and move it in slow circles around your head. It warms up the shoulders and upper back.
Hold an empty barbell or broomstick wide and bring it overhead and back, then return. It opens the shoulders for pressing.
Push your hips back with a straight back and slightly bent knees, and stand up by squeezing your glutes. The base pattern for all hinges.

sjekk.sporsmal: What is the key in a hip hinge?
valg: Push the hips back with a straight back
sjekk.forklaring: The hinge comes from the hips pushing back — the back stays straight, not rounded.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: March easy in place and roll your shoulders for a minute.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Shake it out and breathe calmly.

enheter.intro: The whole secret behind progress in one word: overload. Learn how to manage weight, sets, reps and effort — and how to lift safely.

teori.tittel: A little more, little by little
teori.undertittel: Progressive overload, sets and reps, how hard to push, and safe technique.

avsnitt.p: There's one principle all strength progress rests on: progressive overload. The body adapts to what you demand of it, so to get stronger you have to gradually demand a little more — slightly heavier weight, one more rep, or one more set — over weeks and months. If you do the same every time, you stand still.

A set is a series of reps you do without a break. To build strength and muscle, most people work well in the range of 5–12 reps per set, 2–4 sets per exercise, with a few minutes' rest between the heavy sets. Choose a weight that makes the last reps genuinely challenging, but that you can manage with good technique.

Reps in reserve (RIR) tells you how many reps you had left when you stopped. RIR 2 means you could have done two more. For most people, and for safe training on your own, aim for RIR 1–3 — close to the limit, but not to exhaustion every time. That way you build strength without unnecessary risk.

Good technique starts with bracing your core as if someone were about to punch you in the stomach, and keeping your back in a neutral, strong position. Breathe out at the hardest point. Always start lighter than you think, increase gradually, and stop a set if your form breaks down. Safe, controlled lifting beats heavy, sloppy lifts every time.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is progressive overload?
valg: Gradually asking a little more of the body over time
sporsmal.forklaring: The body adapts to what you demand — increase weight, reps or sets little by little to get stronger.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does RIR 2 mean?
valg: You had two reps left when you stopped
sporsmal.forklaring: RIR = reps in reserve. RIR 2 means two reps left in the tank — close to the limit, but not fully spent.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is most important for a safe back during lifting?
valg: Brace your core and keep your back neutral and strong
Round your back to reach farther
Hold your breath the whole set
sporsmal.forklaring: Brace your core as if against a shove and keep your back neutral — that protects you when it gets heavy.

kilder.kilde: Schoenfeld et al. – volume and progression for strength and muscle growth (J. Strength Cond. Res.)
kilder.om: Research on how sets, reps and gradually increased load drive strength and muscle growth.

kilder.kilde: Dr. Stuart McGill – core bracing and safe lifting technique
kilder.om: Describes bracing (core tension) and a neutral back as the foundation for injury-free lifting.

steg.blurb: Lie on your back with bent knees, press your hips up and squeeze your glutes hard at the top. It wakes up the glutes before you lift.
steg.dose: ×12 with a squeeze at the top

steg.blurb: Hold a dumbbell against your chest, sit down deep and use your elbows to press your knees gently out. It opens the hips for squatting.

sjekk.sporsmal: What is the point of prying goblet squats?
valg: Opening the hips and practicing the deep squat position
sjekk.forklaring: It opens the hips and lets you get used to the deep bottom position before you add weight.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few hip hinges and arm circles.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Shake out your legs and breathe calmly.

undertittel: The body's biggest muscles: learn the squat and lunge with free weights.

enheter.intro: The squat is the king of leg exercises. We build it from the goblet squat to the barbell, and add lunges for single-leg strength.

teori.undertittel: The squat pattern, depth, knees and how to build from dumbbell to barbell.

avsnitt.p: The squat trains the thighs, glutes and core at once and is one of the most valuable exercises there is. The pattern is simple: lower your hips down and slightly back as if toward a chair, keep your chest lifted, and stand up by pressing through your whole foot.

The goblet squat — where you hold a dumbbell or kettlebell against your chest — is the perfect start. The weight in front helps you stay upright and go deep with good form. When the goblet squat feels easy and safe, you can move on to a barbell on your back to load heavier.

Let your knees track over your toes and push slightly out — they shouldn't cave inward. Go as deep as you can with a straight back and your heels on the floor; for most people, thighs parallel to the floor or a little below is a good target. If you have stiff ankles, it helps to raise your heels slightly or work on mobility.

The lunge trains one leg at a time and builds balance, strength and symmetry that the squat doesn't hit as well. The reverse lunge is often kindest to the knees. Keep your torso tall and lower your rear knee gently toward the floor.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why start with the goblet squat?
valg: The weight in front helps you stay upright and go deep safely
sporsmal.forklaring: The goblet weight balances you and teaches good, deep squat form before you move to the barbell.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What should the knees do in a squat?
valg: Track the toes and push slightly outward
sporsmal.forklaring: Let your knees track over your toes and push slightly out — never collapse inward.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is a benefit of lunges?
valg: Trains one leg at a time for balance and symmetry
sporsmal.forklaring: Single-leg work like lunges builds balance and corrects side-to-side imbalances.

kilder.kilde: Dan John – the goblet squat as a foundational exercise
kilder.om: Popularized the goblet squat as the safest way to learn the squat pattern.

kilder.kilde: Mark Rippetoe – “Starting Strength” (barbell squat)
kilder.om: Detailed technique for the barbell squat: depth, knee tracking and a neutral back.

steg.blurb: Hold a dumbbell against your chest, sit down deep between your hips with your chest lifted, and stand up through your whole foot.

sjekk.sporsmal: Where do you hold the weight in a goblet squat?
sjekk.forklaring: Hold the dumbbell/kettlebell against your chest — it balances you and helps you get deep.

steg.blurb: With the barbell resting on your upper back: brace your core, sit down and back with your chest up, and press up through your heels.

sjekk.sporsmal: Where does the barbell rest in a regular squat?
sjekk.forklaring: The barbell rests firmly on the upper back (the trapezius), not on the neck vertebrae.

steg.blurb: With a dumbbell in each hand: step forward, lower your rear knee toward the floor, and press yourself back. Keep your torso tall.
Step back and lower your rear knee gently toward the floor, then stand up through your front leg. Gentle on the knees.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do prying goblet squats and glute bridges to warm up.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Lightly stretch your thighs and hip flexors.

undertittel: The posterior chain and the back: deadlift, Romanian deadlift and rows with free weights.

enheter.intro: The deadlift and rowing exercises build a strong back and posterior chain. It all starts with a good hip hinge — the movement that protects your back.

teori.tittel: Lift from the hips, not the back
teori.undertittel: Hip hinge, deadlift and rows — how to build a strong, safe backside.

avsnitt.p: The hip hinge is perhaps the most important movement to learn: bending and lifting by pushing the hips back and forward, instead of rounding your back. It protects the lower back in everything from deadlifts to picking something up off the floor in daily life.

The Romanian deadlift (RDL) with dumbbells is the best way to learn the hinge. With slightly bent knees you push your hips back and lower the weights along your legs with a straight back, until you feel the stretch in your hamstrings — then you drive your hips forward again. The back stays long and neutral the whole way.

The deadlift from the floor with a barbell is one of the strongest full-body exercises. Set up with the bar over the middle of your foot, grip it, brace your core, lift your chest and press the floor away as you drive your hips in. The bar should slide close along your legs. Start light and build the technique before the weight.

Pressing exercises need a counterweight: rowing. The one-arm dumbbell row (supported on a bench) and the standing bent-over row build the upper back and rear shoulders, improve posture and balance out all the chest pressing. Pull your elbow back and squeeze your shoulder blades together.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the essence of a hip hinge?
valg: Push the hips back and forward with a straight back
Rounding the back to reach down
sporsmal.forklaring: The hinge comes from the hips — a straight, neutral back protects the lower back.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why learn the Romanian deadlift before the barbell deadlift?
valg: It teaches you the hinge safely with a lighter load
sporsmal.forklaring: The RDL with dumbbells is the easiest way into the hinge pattern before you load a barbell from the floor.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does rowing balance out in a program?
valg: All the chest pressing — it builds the upper back and posture
sporsmal.forklaring: Pulling/rowing counters the forward pull of pressing and builds a strong, upright posture.

kilder.kilde: Mark Rippetoe – “Starting Strength” (deadlift)
kilder.om: A thorough walkthrough of the deadlift's setup and safe execution from the floor.

kilder.kilde: Michael Boyle / Dan John – hip hinge and RDL
kilder.om: A practical methodology for learning the hip hinge with the Romanian deadlift before heavy deadlifts.

steg.blurb: Knees slightly bent: push your hips back and lower the dumbbells along your legs with a straight back until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings, then drive your hips forward.

sjekk.sporsmal: Where should you feel a Romanian deadlift?
sjekk.forklaring: You should feel the stretch in the hamstrings — not in the lower back. Keep your back straight.

steg.blurb: Barbell over the middle of your foot: grip it, brace your core, lift your chest and press the floor away as your hips drive in. The bar slides close along your legs.

sjekk.sporsmal: Where should the barbell be before you lift from the floor?
valg: Over the middle of the foot, close to the shin
Far in front of the toes
sjekk.forklaring: Start with the barbell over the middle of your foot and close to your shins — then the lift travels in an efficient, safe line.

steg.blurb: Support yourself on a bench with one hand, let the dumbbell hang, and pull your elbow back and up while you squeeze your shoulder blade.
Hinge forward with a straight back, let the dumbbells hang, and row them up toward your hips by squeezing your shoulder blades together.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do bodyweight hip hinges and cat-cows.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Lightly stretch your hamstrings and back.

undertittel: Push away: bench press, shoulder press and arms with dumbbells and barbell.

enheter.navn: Pressing overhead and from the chest
enheter.intro: The pressing exercises build chest, shoulders and arms. We learn the bench press and shoulder press with both dumbbells and the barbell.

teori.undertittel: Bench press, shoulder press and curl — safe pressing built on stable shoulders.

avsnitt.p: The pressing movements — pushing a weight away from the body — build the chest, shoulders and triceps. The two big ones are the bench press (pressing from the chest, lying down) and the shoulder press (pressing overhead, standing). Both exist with dumbbells and a barbell, and both require stable, set shoulders.

The dumbbell bench press is a good start: each arm works independently, the range of motion is free, and it's easy to drop the weights safely if it gets too heavy. Once your technique is solid, the barbell lets you load heavier. Always use safety arms or a spotter when you bench heavy with a barbell.

The key to safe pressing is to set your shoulders: draw your shoulder blades slightly together and down toward your hips, and keep them there. That gives a stable platform to press from and protects the shoulder joint. Lower the weight with control and press up without locking your elbows hard.

The big pressing exercises already train the arms well, but an isolated biceps curl at the end builds extra arm strength and balances out the triceps work from the presses. Keep your elbows tucked to your sides and lift with control — no swinging from your back.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why start with the dumbbell bench press?
valg: Free range, each arm independently, and safe to drop
sporsmal.forklaring: Dumbbells allow free movement and are safe to set down — a good way in before the barbell.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does it mean to set the shoulders in a press?
valg: Pulling the shoulder blades back and down for a stable platform
Raising the shoulders toward the ears
sporsmal.forklaring: Set your shoulders (back and down) for a steady, safe platform to press from.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How do you do a clean biceps curl?
valg: Elbows tucked to the sides, lift with control without swinging
Swing the weight up from your back
Use your whole body to throw it up
sporsmal.forklaring: Keep your elbows in and curl with control — if you swing from your back, you're cheating and risking injury.

kilder.kilde: American College of Sports Medicine – resistance training guidelines
kilder.om: A framework for safe pressing technique and a balanced program between pushing and pulling.

kilder.kilde: Mark Rippetoe – “Starting Strength” (bench press and overhead press)
kilder.om: Technique for the bench press and standing press with a barbell, including shoulder setup and safety.

steg.blurb: Lie on a bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Set your shoulders, lower the weights to chest height and press up without locking your elbows hard.

sjekk.sporsmal: What do you do with your shoulders in the bench press?
valg: Sets them back and down for a stable platform
Raises them up toward the ears
sjekk.forklaring: Set your shoulder blades back and down — that gives a safe, strong platform to press from.

steg.blurb: On a bench with a barbell: set your shoulders, lower the bar to your chest with control and press up. Use safety arms or a spotter.
Stand tall with a braced core, dumbbells at shoulder height, and press them straight up overhead without arching your back.

sjekk.sporsmal: What keeps you from arching your back during the shoulder press?
valg: A braced core and tall posture
sjekk.forklaring: Brace your core and stand tall — then you press from a stable column without straining the lower back.

steg.blurb: Stand tall with your elbows tucked to your sides and curl the dumbbells up with control, without swinging from your back. Lower slowly.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do kettlebell halos and shoulder rotations with an empty barbell.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Lightly stretch your chest and shoulders.

undertittel: Explosive hips, strong grip and full-body control: kettlebell, carries and the Turkish get-up.

enheter.intro: The most complete exercises last: the kettlebell swing for explosive power, carries for grip and core, and the Turkish get-up for full control.

teori.undertittel: Kettlebell swings, carries and the Turkish get-up — the most complete free-weight exercises.

avsnitt.p: Finally we build power and full-body control: the ability to produce speed, hold heavy loads and steer the body through compound movements. This is strength that truly transfers to sport and daily life.

avsnitt.h: The swing is an explosive hinge

avsnitt.p: The kettlebell swing is a powerful hip hinge: you snap your hips back and drive them explosively forward, so the kettlebell floats up on its own. The power comes from the hips, not the arms — the arms just go along for the ride. It builds explosive strength in the posterior chain and a robust core.

The farmer's carry — walking with heavy dumbbells or kettlebells in your hands — is one of the simplest and most effective exercises there is. It builds grip, core stability, shoulders and a strong posture, all while you just walk. Stand tall, brace your core and walk under control.

avsnitt.h: The Turkish get-up — the crown jewel

avsnitt.p: The Turkish get-up is a slow, compound journey from lying to standing with a weight in a straight arm overhead. It trains stability, mobility and control in the whole body at once. Learn it first without weight, then with a light kettlebell, one calm step at a time.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Where does the power in a kettlebell swing come from?
valg: From the hips driving explosively forward
sporsmal.forklaring: The swing is driven by an explosive hip hinge — the arms just go along for the ride.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does the farmer's carry build?
sporsmal.forklaring: Walking with a heavy load builds grip, core stability, shoulders and a strong, upright posture.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How should you learn the Turkish get-up?
valg: First without weight, then with a light kettlebell, step by step
As heavy as possible right away
sporsmal.forklaring: It's complex — learn each step without weight first, then add a light kettlebell.

kilder.kilde: Pavel Tsatsouline / StrongFirst – kettlebell swing and Turkish get-up
kilder.om: Reference methodology for the hardstyle swing and get-up: power from the hips and safe progression.

kilder.kilde: Dan John – carries (loaded carries)
kilder.om: Popularized the farmer's carry as a simple, highly effective exercise for grip, core and posture.

steg.blurb: Snap your hips back and drive them explosively forward so the kettlebell floats up to chest height. The power is in your hips, not your arm.

sjekk.sporsmal: What drives the kettlebell swing?
sjekk.forklaring: The hips snap back and drive forward — that throws the kettlebell up. The arm only guides it.

steg.blurb: Hold a heavy dumbbell in each hand, stand tall with a braced core, and walk under control. Grip, core and posture all at once.
Rise from lying to standing with a weight in a straight arm overhead, one controlled step at a time — and back down again. Learn without weight first.

sjekk.sporsmal: What characterizes the Turkish get-up?
valg: A slow, compound journey from lying to standing with the weight overhead
sjekk.forklaring: It's a controlled full-body journey from the floor to standing with the weight in a straight arm — stability and control.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do hip hinges and a few light swing rehearsals without weight.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Walk easy and shake out your grip.

undertittel: What high-intensity interval training is, why short efforts have such a big effect, and your first movements.

enheter.intro: HIIT gives a lot in a short time — but only if you understand the alternation between hard and rest. We start with the principle and two simple heart-rate drivers.

teori.undertittel: What high-intensity interval training is — and why a few minutes can do so much.

avsnitt.p: HIIT stands for high-intensity interval training: short bouts of hard work, alternated with rests. Instead of jogging steadily for an hour, you alternate between really pushing for a few seconds and recovering again. It's one of the most time-efficient ways to train cardio — and this is the first lesson in the HIIT school.

Because the work intervals are short, you can work much harder than you could sustain continuously. It's exactly that high effort that raises your fitness (VO2 max) quickly. The rest between intervals lets you recover enough to go all-out again in the next one.

After a hard HIIT session, the body uses extra energy for quite a while as it recovers — this is called EPOC, or the afterburn. Together with the strong cardiovascular demand during the session itself, this means that a few short minutes deliver a surprisingly large training effect.

Hard doesn't mean uncontrolled. Always warm up first, learn the movements at an easy pace before you turn up the speed, and keep good technique even when you get tired. If you're new or have health challenges, start with low-intensity intervals and build yourself up. Better to do one solid interval with good form than ten sloppy ones.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the essence of HIIT?
valg: Alternating between short, hard work intervals and rests
Steady, easy training for a long time
sporsmal.forklaring: HIIT alternates between hard work and rest — that alternation lets you work harder than you otherwise could.

sporsmal.sporsmal: Why can you work harder in an interval than continuously?
valg: Because the effort is short and you get a rest afterward
sporsmal.forklaring: Short efforts with rest between them let you push far harder than you could sustain in one continuous stretch.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is EPOC?
valg: Extra energy expenditure while the body recovers after the session
sporsmal.forklaring: EPOC is the afterburn — the body uses extra energy for a while after a hard session.

kilder.kilde: Gibala et al. – low-volume HIIT (McMaster University, 2010–2012)
kilder.om: Shows that small amounts of high-intensity interval training deliver large fitness and health benefits.

kilder.kilde: American College of Sports Medicine – HIIT guidelines
kilder.om: Recommends warming up, gradual progression and good technique, especially for beginners.

steg.blurb: Take one hard work interval of 20–40 seconds where you really push, and rest afterward. Feel how the alternation works.

sjekk.sporsmal: How hard should a HIIT work interval be?
valg: Near maximal — your breathing gets heavy and speech breaks up
Easy enough that you can sing
sjekk.forklaring: In the work interval you give near-maximal effort — it's the high effort that produces the result.

steg.blurb: Run in place with your knees driving up to hip height at a fast pace. Use your arms actively.
Jump your feet out to the sides while your arms go overhead, and gather back in at a steady, fast rhythm.

sjekk.sporsmal: How do you make jumping jacks easier?
valg: Step-jacks — step one leg out at a time without jumping
sjekk.forklaring: Step-jacks (without the jump) give the same movement gently on the joints and knees.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: March easy and roll your shoulders for two minutes.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Walk easy until your heart rate drops and breathe deeply.

undertittel: The toolbox: the explosive jumps and full-body movements that make HIIT tough and effective.

enheter.intro: Jumps are the engine of HIIT: squat jumps and lunge jumps build explosive power and raise your heart rate fast. The key is to land softly.

teori.tittel: Jump with power, land with control
teori.undertittel: Plyometric jumps build explosive power — here's how to do them safely.

avsnitt.p: Jumping exercises (plyometrics) train the muscles to produce force quickly. They raise your heart rate in seconds and build explosive strength in the legs — perfect for HIIT. But because they load the joints and tendons, technique is extra important.

The secret to safe jumping is a soft landing: land on the balls of your feet and roll down toward the heel, with bent knees that spring and cushion the impact. Think silent landing. If you land stiff-legged or with your knees caving inward, the load increases unnecessarily.

Explosive jumps give the most when each jump is controlled. It's better to do ten good, powerful jumps with a soft landing than twenty sloppy ones. If your technique gets bad when you're tired, take a break — that's when injuries happen.

If jumping is too much for your joints today, there's always a gentler variation: swap squat jumps for fast, deep squats and jumping lunges for alternating lunges without the jump. You get almost the same heart-rate effect without the impact — HIIT should be adapted to you.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is most important for safe jumping?
valg: A soft landing with bent knees to cushion it
sporsmal.forklaring: Land softly on the balls of your feet with knees bent to absorb the shock — a silent landing protects the joints.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What do you do if jumping is too much for your knees?
valg: Switch to fast squats / lunges without the jump
sporsmal.forklaring: Gentle no-jump variations give almost the same heart-rate effect without the impact.

sporsmal.sporsmal: When do plyometric injuries most often happen?
valg: When your form breaks down because you're tired
sporsmal.forklaring: Take a break when your form breaks down from fatigue — that's when the landings become dangerous.

kilder.kilde: NSCA – guidelines for plyometric training
kilder.om: Describes safe plyometrics: soft landings, control and gradual progression.

kilder.kilde: ACSM – HIIT and injury prevention
kilder.om: Recommends low-impact variations and good landing technique to train hard safely.

steg.blurb: Lower into a squat and explode up into a jump. Land softly with bent knees and go straight down into the next.

sjekk.sporsmal: How do you land a squat jump?
valg: Softly on the balls of your feet with bent knees
sjekk.forklaring: Land softly with bent knees to cushion the impact — think silent landing.

steg.blurb: From a lunge: jump up and switch your legs in the air, land softly in a new lunge. Keep your torso tall.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few easy squats and glute bridges to warm up.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Walk easy and lightly stretch your thighs.

enheter.intro: The toughest HIIT movements use the whole body: burpees and mountain climbers. Here you'll learn the technique that keeps you safe when it burns.

teori.tittel: The whole body in one move
teori.undertittel: Burpees and mountain climbers — maximum effect, and how to keep your form when you get tired.

avsnitt.p: The most effective HIIT movements use the whole body at once. The burpee and mountain climber drive your heart rate through the roof and train legs, core and upper body all together — that's why they're HIIT classics. They're also the ones whose form breaks down most easily when you get tired.

A burpee is really four movements in one: hands down, jump your legs back to a plank, jump them in again, and up into a hop. Learn each step on its own at an easy pace first. Keep your back stable in the plank part — don't let your hips sag — and land softly from the hop.

avsnitt.h: Mountain climbers with a steady core

avsnitt.p: In the mountain climber you hold a steady plank while your legs run in and out. The key is to keep your hips low and your core braced so your hips don't bounce up. Start slow and increase the pace once you can keep the plank still.

With full-body movements it's tempting to chase speed at the expense of form. Flip it: keep good technique, and let the speed come over time. A calm, clean burpee beats a fast, collapsed one — both for effect and for your back.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What should you watch for in the plank part of a burpee?
valg: Keep your back stable — don't let your hips sag
Round the back as much as possible
sporsmal.forklaring: Hold a stable, straight body in the plank — sagging hips strain the lower back.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the key in the mountain climber?
valg: Keep the hips low and the core braced so the plank stays still
sporsmal.forklaring: Keep your hips low and your core braced — the plank should stay still while the legs run.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What should come first, form or speed?
sporsmal.forklaring: Prioritize technique; let the speed build up once your form is solid, especially as you get tired.

kilder.kilde: ACSM – full-body HIIT and technique
kilder.om: Emphasizes good body control in compound movements like burpees to train safely.

kilder.kilde: Dr. Stuart McGill – spinal stability in the plank
kilder.om: The principle of a stable, neutral back in plank-based movements.

steg.blurb: Hands on the floor, jump your legs back to a plank, jump them in again and up into a hop. Keep your back stable and land softly.

sjekk.sporsmal: How do you make burpees easier?
valg: Drop the jump and the push-up — step out and in slowly
sjekk.forklaring: Without the jump and push-up, at an easy pace, you get the movement gently — build up from there.

steg.blurb: In a steady plank, drive your knees alternately and quickly in toward your chest. Keep your hips low and your core braced.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Do a few cat-cows and an easy plank to warm up.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Rest in child's pose and breathe calmly.

undertittel: The named recipes: Tabata, 30-20-10 and EMOM — how to structure a HIIT session.

enheter.intro: Now we put the movements into a system. Learn the classic HIIT recipes, so you can control the intensity yourself and build your own sessions.

teori.undertittel: Tabata, 30-20-10 and EMOM — how they're built, and how to dose them.

avsnitt.p: A HIIT session needs a structure that governs when you work and when you rest. Over the years, researchers and coaches have created some well-known recipes — protocols — that make it easy to hit the right load. If you know a couple of them, you can build a good session anywhere with just a clock.

The most famous is Tabata: 20 seconds of maximal work, 10 seconds of rest, repeated 8 times — four brutal minutes. It comes from a 1996 Japanese study on very hard cycling. One Tabata block per exercise is enough; take a full rest before you do another.

The 30-20-10 method splits each minute into 30 seconds easy, 20 moderate and 10 all-out — easy to manage and easy to like. EMOM (every minute on the minute) gives you a fixed number of reps to do at the start of each minute; the rest of the minute is your break. The faster you work, the more you rest.

Whatever the protocol, the same rule applies: start with fewer rounds and an exercise you're comfortable with, and build up the number of rounds, the pace or harder movements over time. One to three hard HIIT sessions a week is plenty for most people — the body needs rest days to get stronger.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the Tabata recipe?
sporsmal.forklaring: Tabata is 20 seconds of maximal work and 10 seconds of rest, repeated 8 times — four minutes.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What does EMOM mean?
valg: A fixed number of reps at the start of each minute, the rest is rest
Training the whole minute without a break
sporsmal.forklaring: EMOM = every minute on the minute: do the reps, and the rest of the minute is your break.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How many hard HIIT sessions per week suit most people?
valg: One to three, with rest days in between
sporsmal.forklaring: 1–3 hard sessions a week is plenty — the body gets stronger on the rest days.

kilder.kilde: Tabata et al. – 20/10 intervals (Med. Sci. Sports Exerc., 1996)
kilder.om: The original study behind the Tabata protocol: four minutes of very hard interval training.

kilder.kilde: Gunnarsson & Bangsbo – the 10-20-30 concept (University of Copenhagen, 2012)
kilder.om: Shows that the 30-20-10 structure improves fitness and health on reduced training volume.

steg.blurb: Choose one exercise you're comfortable with. Work all-out for 20 seconds, rest for 10, and repeat for 8 rounds — four minutes total.

sjekk.sporsmal: How many rounds is a Tabata?
sjekk.forklaring: Eight rounds of 20 sec work and 10 sec rest — four minutes in total.

steg.blurb: Split each minute into 30 sec easy, 20 sec moderate and 10 sec all-out. Repeat five times, then a rest.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: March easy and do a few jumping jacks to warm up.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Walk easy until your heart rate drops and breathe deeply.

undertittel: How to train with your bike: the intensity zones, the easy base ride and active rest.

enheter.intro: Good cycling training is about managing your effort. Learn the zones, the easy base ride that builds your engine, and how to recover.

teori.tittel: Train with your head, not just your legs
teori.undertittel: The intensity zones, FTP and why most of your rides should be easy.

avsnitt.p: Cycling is one of the gentlest and most effective forms of cardio there is — joint-friendly, and easy to dose very precisely. But to get better, you have to manage your effort. The key is intensity zones. This is the first lesson in the cycling school.

We divide effort into zones from Z1 to Z5. Z1–Z2 is easy endurance where you can talk; Z3–Z4 is comfortably hard around threshold; Z5 is short-lived maximum. Each zone has its job, and a good cycling plan uses them deliberately instead of pedaling equally hard every time.

If you have a power meter, the zones are calculated from FTP — the highest steady power (watts) you can hold for about an hour. Without a meter you can steer just as well by heart rate and feel. The point is the same: knowing when you're easy, when you're at threshold, and when you're giving it all.

Research on endurance athletes shows a clear, polarized distribution: around 80% of the time is easy, and only a small part is hard. The easy base ride builds the large aerobic engine, and makes the few hard sessions actually work. The most common mistake is riding the base rides too hard.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is FTP?
valg: The highest steady power you can hold for about an hour
sporsmal.forklaring: FTP (functional threshold power) is your threshold power — the basis your zones are calculated from.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How should training be distributed?
sporsmal.forklaring: The polarized split — mostly easy, a little hard — builds fitness best.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the most common mistake on base rides?
sporsmal.forklaring: Most people ride their easy rides too hard. Hold back — the base ride should be comfortable.

kilder.kilde: Allen & Coggan – “Training and Racing with a Power Meter”
kilder.om: The standard reference on power-based training: FTP, intensity zones and structured sessions.

kilder.kilde: Seiler – polarized training distribution (2010)
kilder.om: Shows that endurance athletes train mostly in the low zone and only a small share hard.

steg.blurb: Feel the difference between the zones: talk freely in the easy zone, short phrases when it's hard, single words at maximum.
steg.dose: Test the zones for a few minutes

sjekk.sporsmal: How do you know you're in the easy zone?
valg: You can talk in full sentences
You can only get single words out
sjekk.forklaring: Easy zone = you can talk freely. If your speech gets choppy, you're moving up through the zones.

steg.blurb: Ride steadily and comfortably in zone 2 where you can talk, with a round cadence around 85–95 rpm.

sjekk.sporsmal: How hard is an easy base ride?
valg: Easy enough to hold a conversation
sjekk.forklaring: The base ride is talking pace in zone 2 — it feels almost too easy, and that's the point.

steg.blurb: Pedal very lightly in zone 1 with low resistance — active rest that moves blood without loading the legs.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Pedal easy for five minutes and build up gradually.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Spin down easy and breathe deeply.

undertittel: A round, efficient pedal stroke: the right cadence, spin-ups and the power of climbing.

enheter.intro: How you pedal matters as much as how hard. Learn a round pedal stroke, find the right cadence, and tame the hills.

teori.undertittel: What cadence is, why a round pedal stroke pays off, and how to ride uphill.

avsnitt.p: Two riders can hold the same speed in completely different ways. The difference lies in the pedal stroke: how smoothly and round you pedal, and which cadence (pedaling frequency) you choose. Good technique spares your legs and gives more speed for the effort.

Cadence is how many revolutions you pedal per minute (rpm). Most people ride most efficiently around 85–95 rpm on flat ground. A lower cadence in a heavy gear loads the muscles more; a higher cadence shifts the load toward breathing and heart. Being able to switch is a skill in itself.

Aim for a smooth, round pedal stroke all the way around the circle — not just stamping down. A helpful image: imagine you're scraping mud off your shoe at the bottom of the stroke. Spin-ups, where you gradually increase your cadence to 110–120+ rpm in a light gear without bouncing in the saddle, build exactly this smoothness.

On a climb, cadence drops and power rises. Choose a gear that lets you hold steady power without fully grinding, and sit calmly with a steady torso. Stand up when it gets steep or your legs need a change — standing pedaling gives more power but costs more, so use it in doses.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is an efficient base cadence on flat ground for most people?
sporsmal.forklaring: Most people ride most efficiently around 85–95 rpm; lower loads the muscles more, higher taxes breathing and heart.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What do you practice with spin-ups?
valg: A round, smooth pedal stroke at high cadence
sporsmal.forklaring: Spin-ups build a smooth, round pedal stroke at high cadence without bouncing in the saddle.

sporsmal.sporsmal: When should you stand up on a climb?
valg: When it gets steep or your legs need a change
sporsmal.forklaring: Standing pedaling gives more power but costs more — use it in doses, not constantly.

kilder.kilde: Allen & Coggan – “Training and Racing with a Power Meter” (cadence)
kilder.om: Describes the role of cadence and how pedaling frequency affects load and efficiency.

kilder.kilde: USA Cycling / coaching literature – pedaling technique and spin-ups
kilder.om: Practical drills for a round pedal stroke and safe high-cadence technique.

steg.blurb: Find your base cadence around 85–95 rpm, and practice spin-ups: build smoothly up to 110–120+ rpm in a light gear without bouncing.

sjekk.sporsmal: What is a sign that your cadence is too high for your control?
sjekk.forklaring: If you bounce in the saddle, your cadence is higher than your control — lower it a bit and build up.

steg.blurb: Ride in a heavy gear / uphill with a slow, powerful cadence (60–75 rpm), and stand up in doses when it gets steep.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Pedal easy and do a couple of spin-ups to warm up.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Spin down easy in a light gear.

undertittel: Speed and form: threshold, sweet spot and the hard VO2 efforts that raise your ceiling.

enheter.intro: The hard sessions that make you faster: the controlled threshold you can hold for a long time, and the brutal VO2 efforts. Dose them sensibly.

teori.undertittel: Threshold and sweet spot, VO2 intervals, and why they're only a small part of training.

avsnitt.p: Once the foundation of easy rides is in place, it's the hard sessions that raise the ceiling for how fast and how long you can ride. But hard means controlled: the best interval sessions are in fact very structured.

Threshold pace is the speed you can just barely hold for a long time — comfortably hard, around your FTP. The sweet spot sits just below it (88–95% of FTP) and gives a lot of training effect for relatively little wear. Threshold and sweet-spot blocks of 8–20 minutes are the cornerstone of many cycling plans.

VO2 intervals are short, hard efforts in zone 5 — for example 4×4 minutes, or short 30/15 efforts where you alternate 30 seconds hard and 15 seconds easy. They raise your maximal oxygen uptake (VO2 max) quickly, but they're very demanding, so one or two such sessions a week is enough.

Hard intervals only work if you're rested enough to do them well, and let your body recover afterward. Always put a thorough warm-up first, hold steady effort throughout each interval instead of emptying yourself at the start, and let the easy rides fill the rest of the week.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is the sweet spot?
valg: Just below threshold (88–95% FTP) — a lot of effect for the wear
sporsmal.forklaring: The sweet spot sits just below threshold and gives a lot of training effect for relatively little wear.

sporsmal.sporsmal: What is a 30/15 interval?
sporsmal.forklaring: 30/15 are short VO2 micro-intervals: 30 sec hard and 15 sec easy, repeated many times.

sporsmal.sporsmal: How many hard VO2 sessions per week are appropriate?
sporsmal.forklaring: VO2 intervals are very demanding — one or two a week is enough, the rest should be easy.

kilder.kilde: Rønnestad et al. – 30/15 micro-intervals
kilder.om: Research showing that short 30/15 VO2 intervals raise fitness effectively.

kilder.kilde: Helgerud, Hoff et al. – 4×4 intervals (CERG/NTNU, 2007)
kilder.om: Shows that hard 4-minute intervals raise maximal oxygen uptake.

steg.blurb: Ride steadily and comfortably hard at around 88–95% of threshold. Keep the effort stable throughout the interval.

sjekk.sporsmal: What does the right threshold pace feel like?
valg: Comfortably hard and steady — a few words at a time
An all-out sprint from the start
sjekk.forklaring: Threshold is steadily comfortably hard. If you start too hard, your speed drops — settle just below and hold.

steg.blurb: After a good warm-up: do hard efforts in zone 5 (e.g. 4 min, or 30/15) with steady, high intensity, and take a full rest.

sjekk.sporsmal: What's smart to do before hard VO2 intervals?
sjekk.forklaring: Hard effort requires a warm body — a thorough warm-up gives better, safer intervals.

uteksaminering.oppvarming: Pedal easy for ten minutes with a couple of spin-ups.
uteksaminering.nedtrapping: Spin down easy for five minutes.
